  "limit": 80,
  "offset": 44
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "/**\n * In-memory implementation of {@link Records} backed by a ByteBuffer.\n *\n * <p><b>Primary Use Cases:</b>\n * <ul>\n *   <li>Reading or modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches from the log or network.</li>\n *   <li>Iterating over batches and records for filtering, validation, or processing.</li>\n * </ul>\n * To create new records from scratch, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or factory methods like\n * {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}, {@link #withIdempotentRecords}, etc.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> This class is <b>not thread-safe</b>. The backing ByteBuffer and batch\n * objects can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates during log appending). External synchronization\n * is required for concurrent access. However, individual batch iteration is safe if the buffer contents\n * are not modified during iteration.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b>\n * <ul>\n *   <li>Batch iteration is lazy\u2014batches are parsed incrementally, not all at once.</li>\n *   <li>Record decompression is lazy\u2014only decompressed when actually read via iterators.</li>\n *   <li>No copying of record data; all reads directly reference the backing buffer via slices.</li>\n *   <li>{@link #validBytes()} requires scanning all batches; the result is cached.</li>\n * </ul></p>\n *\n * <p><b>Buffer Management:</b> The ByteBuffer backing this instance can be in several states:\n * <ul>\n *   <li><b>Read-only:</b> Created via {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)}, position/limit managed externally.</li>\n *   <li><b>Writable:</b> Created via {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder#build()}, position and limit reflect valid data.</li>\n * </ul></p>\n *\n * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches\n * @see RecordBatch for batch-level operations\n * @see Record for record-level operations\n */\npublic class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {\n    public static final MemoryRecords EMPTY = MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer.allocate(0));\n\n    private final ByteBuffer buffer;\n\n    private final Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches = this::batchIterator;\n\n    private int validBytes = -1;\n\n    // Construct a writable memory records\n    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {\n        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, \"buffer should not be null\");\n        this.buffer = buffer;\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int sizeInBytes() {\n        return buffer.limit();\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int writeTo(TransferableChannel channel, int position, int length) throws IOException {\n        if (((long) position) + length > buffer.limit())\n            throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"position+length should not be greater than buffer.limit(), position: \"\n                    + position + \", length: \" + length + \", buffer.limit(): \" + buffer.limit());\n\n        return Utils.tryWriteTo(channel, position, length, buffer);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Write all records to the given channel (including partial records).\n     * @param channel The channel to write to\n     * @return The number of bytes written\n     * @throws IOException For any IO errors writing to the channel\n     */\n    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {\n        buffer.mark();\n        int written = 0;\n        while (written < sizeInBytes())\n            written += channel.write(buffer);\n        buffer.reset();\n        return written;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * The total number of bytes in this record collection that form complete, valid batches.",
    "filePath": "/workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 44,
    "totalLines": 968
  },
  "type": "text"
}

  "file": {
    "content": "# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Summary\n\nThis document summarizes the Javadoc additions made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`.\n\n## Overview\n\nThree core classes handle record batch serialization in Kafka:\n1. **RecordBatch** - Interface defining batch operations\n2. **DefaultRecordBatch** - Concrete implementation for magic v2+\n3. **MemoryRecords** - In-memory collection and management of batches\n\n## Class-Level Documentation Enhancements\n\n### RecordBatch (Interface)\n\n**Purpose:** Container and interface for Kafka message batches across all format versions.\n\n**Key Additions:**\n- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly documented as NOT thread-safe (requires external synchronization)\n- **Role in Protocol:** Explained fundamental importance in wire protocol and commit log format\n- **Performance Characteristics:**\n  - Lazy decompression for compressed batches (on-demand via iterators)\n  - Direct buffer access for uncompressed data (no copying)\n  - Enables idempotent producers, transactions, and log compaction features\n\n**Cross-References:**\n- {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for magic v2+ implementation\n- {@link MemoryRecords} for batch collections\n- {@link Record} for individual records\n\n### DefaultRecordBatch (Concrete Implementation)\n\n**Purpose:** Binary serialization and parsing of record batches in Kafka's v2+ format.\n\n**Key Additions:**\n- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; state can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates)\n- **Binary Format:** Documented full schema with field offsets and sizes\n- **CRC Computation:**\n  - Uses CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial\n  - Covers attributes field through end of batch\n  - Partition leader epoch excluded to avoid recomputation\n- **Log Compaction Semantics:** Explained preservation of first/last offset/sequence across compaction\n- **Performance Characteristics:**\n  - Header fields efficiently accessible without iteration\n  - Compressed batches use lazy decompression\n  - Record count efficiently retrievable via countOrNull()\n\n**Key Methods Enhanced:**\n- `baseTimestamp()` - Explains role in timestamp delta calculation and delete horizon\n- `recordInputStream()` - Documents buffer requirements for decompression\n- `streamingIterator()` - Performance impact of buffer allocation and reuse\n\n### MemoryRecords (In-Memory Collection)\n\n**Purpose:** ByteBuffer-backed collection and filtering of record batches.\n\n**Key Additions:**\n- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; backing buffer modified in-place, requires synchronization\n- **Use Cases:** Reading/modifying log buffers, network data, batch collections\n- **Performance Characteristics:**\n  - Lazy batch iteration (parsed incrementally)\n  - Lazy decompression (on-demand during record read)\n  - No copying of record data; direct buffer slicing\n  - Caching of validBytes() after first computation\n- **Buffer States:** Documents read-only vs. writable modes\n\n**Key Methods Enhanced:**\n- `readableRecords()` - Factory method for reading existing batches\n- `validBytes()` - Explains partial batch handling and caching behavior\n- `filterTo()` - Documents log compaction filtering, batch/record retention logic\n- `withRecords()` - Convenience factory for creating simple non-idempotent batches\n- `builder()` - Creates builders for constructing new batches\n\n## Method-Level Documentation\n\n### Critical Method: RecordBatch.streamingIterator()\n\nEnhanced with performance guidance:\n- **Memory Efficiency:** Delays decompression until records requested\n- **Buffer Allocation:** Notes that small batches allocate large buffers (64 KB for LZ4)",
    "filePath": "/workspace/documentation.md",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 1,
    "totalLines": 226
  },

RecordBatch.java\n- **Enhanced Class-Level Javadoc:**\n  - Added thread-safety statement: \"NOT thread-safe\"\n  - Documented role in Kafka protocol\n  - Explained performance characteristics (lazy decompression)\n  - Added cross-references to DefaultRecordBatch, MemoryRecords, Record\n\n- **Enhanced Method Documentation:**\n  - `isValid()` / `ensureValid()`: CRC-32C checksum explanation\n  - `baseOffset()`: Performance implications and iteration cost\n  - `streamingIterator()`: Buffer management, decompression costs, reusable buffers\n\n### 2. DefaultRecordBatch.java\n- **Comprehensive Class-Level Javadoc:**\n  - Full binary schema with field types and annotations\n  - Thread-safety: \"NOT thread-safe\" with in-place modification details\n  - CRC-32C computation: polynomial, coverage, partition leader epoch exclusion\n  - Log compaction semantics: sequence preservation, empty batch retention\n  - Performance characteristics: header field accessibility, lazy decompression\n\n- **Enhanced Method Documentation:**\n  - `baseTimestamp()`: Timestamp delta calculation, delete horizon semantics, empty batch behavior\n  - `recordInputStream()`: Decompression buffer requirements and reusability guidance\n\n### 3. MemoryRecords.java\n- **Detailed Class-Level Javadoc:**\n  - Primary use cases: reading logs, network buffers, filtering\n  - Thread-safety: \"NOT thread-safe\" with synchronization boundaries\n  - Performance characteristics: lazy batch/record iteration, no copying, caching\n  - Buffer management: read-only vs writable modes\n\n- **Enhanced Factory Methods:**\n  - `readableRecords()`: Purpose, buffer lifetime, exception handling\n  - `builder()`: Configuration defaults, convenience for current magic version\n  - `withRecords()`: Convenience factory for simple non-idempotent batches\n\n- **Enhanced Instance Methods:**\n  - `validBytes()`: Partial batch semantics, cache behavior, performance cost\n  - `filterTo()`: Log compaction process, batch/record retention logic, decompression details\n\n## Documentation Quality Standards Met\n\n\u2713 **Thread-Safety (100% Coverage)**\n  - Every class explicitly states thread-safety guarantee\n  - Synchronized access requirements specified\n  - Exception: Batch iteration is safe without sync if buffer not modified\n\n\u2713 **Performance Documentation**\n  - Lazy decompression and iteration patterns documented\n  - Buffer allocation costs explained for decompression\n  - Caching behavior and trade-offs noted\n  - BufferSupplier guidance for optimization provided\n\n\u2713 **Kafka Protocol Context**\n  - Producer ID/epoch for idempotence explained\n  - Sequence numbers for duplicate detection documented\n  - CRC-32C for corruption detection detailed\n  - Offset/sequence preservation during compaction explained\n\n\u2713 **Cross-References**\n  - 1. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
After
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format.
 *
 * <p>In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record
 * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic
 * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External
 * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and
 * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with
 * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features
 * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are
 * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible
 * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>
 *
 * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation
 * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections
 * @see Record Individual record in a batch
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
2. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
After
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format.
 *
 * <p>In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record
 * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic
 * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External
 * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and
 * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with
 * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features
 * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are
 * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible
 * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>
 *
 * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation
 * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections
 * @see Record Individual record in a batch
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
3. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct.
     *
     * @return true If so, false otherwise
     */
    boolean isValid();

    /**
     * Raise an exception if the checksum is not valid.
     */
    void ensureValid();
After
    /**
     * Check whether the CRC-32C checksum of this batch is correct.
     *
     * <p>The checksum covers the batch header (from attributes byte onwards) and all record data.
     * The partition leader epoch field is excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation
     * when the field is assigned by the broker.</p>
     *
     * @return true if the computed checksum matches the stored checksum, false otherwise
     * @see #checksum() to retrieve the stored checksum value
     */
    boolean isValid();

    /**
     * Raise an exception if the checksum of this batch is not valid.
     *
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the stored checksum does not match the computed checksum
     * @see #isValid() for non-exceptional validation
     */
    void ensureValid();
4. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will
     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will
     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return
     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the
     * behavior is equivalent.
     *
     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with
     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.
     *
     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record
     *         as described above).
     */
    long baseOffset();
After
    /**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch.
     *
     * <p>For magic version prior to 2, this returns the offset of the first record in the batch,
     * which generally requires deep iteration. For magic version 2 and above, this returns the
     * first offset of the original record batch (prior to compaction), which is stored directly in
     * the batch header and is efficiently accessible. For non-compacted topics, the behavior is
     * equivalent across versions.</p>
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> For older magic versions (v0, v1), this method requires iterating
     * through records and should be used with caution. {@link #lastOffset()} is safer and more
     * efficient for all magic versions.</p>
     *
     * @return the base offset of this record batch
     * @see #lastOffset() for the more efficiently accessible last offset
     */
    long baseOffset();
5. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
After
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator that delays decompression of the record stream until records are
     * actually requested via {@link Iterator#next()}.
     *
     * <p>If the message format does not support streaming iteration (e.g., legacy formats), the
     * normal iterator is returned instead. This method enables memory-efficient processing of
     * compressed batches by decompressing records on-demand rather than upfront.</p>
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, this avoids allocating large
     * decompression buffers upfront. For small record batches, the cost of allocating a buffer
     * (e.g., 64 KB for LZ4) can dominate decompression cost. Using a {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}
     * that reuses buffers across multiple batches can significantly reduce allocation overhead.</p>
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier of ByteBuffer(s) for decompression. Using a
     *        reusable buffer supplier has significant performance impact for small batches.
     *        {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} creates a new buffer per decompression.
     *
     * @return a closeable iterator over records in this batch. The caller must close this iterator
     *         to release decompression resources.
     *
     * @see BufferSupplier for buffer management strategies
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
6. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence e
... (truncated)
After
/**
 * Concrete implementation of {@link RecordBatch} for magic version 2 and above.
 *
 * <p><b>Binary Format:</b> Records in magic v2+ follow the schema below:
 * <pre>
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32 (CRC-32C from attributes to end of batch)
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64 (timestamp of first record, or delete horizon if flag set)
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64 (or -1 if not set)
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 * </pre>
 * When compression is enabled, compressed record data follows directly after the count field.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> This class is <b>not thread-safe</b>. Batch state can be modified in-place
 * (e.g., via {@link #setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)}) and must be externally synchronized for
 * concurrent access.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>CRC Computation:</b> The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial covers all bytes from the attributes
 * field through the end of the batch. The partition leader epoch field is excluded to avoid CRC
 * recomputation when assigned by the broker. The magic byte must be parsed before interpreting bytes
 * between batch length and CRC.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Log Compaction Semantics:</b> Magic v2+ preserves the first and last offset/sequence numbers
 * from the original batch during log cleaning. This ensures producer state can be correctly restored
 * after leader failover. Base sequence is needed for duplicate detection. Empty batches may be retained
 * to preserve producer state until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b>
 * <ul>
 
... (truncated)
7. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.
     *
     * @return The base timestamp
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
After
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch, which is used to calculate individual record timestamps
     * from their timestamp deltas.
     *
     * <p>The base timestamp semantics depend on context:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For normal batches, this is the timestamp of the first record.</li>
     *   <li>For empty batches (after compaction), this remains the timestamp of the original first
     *       record.</li>
     *   <li>If the delete horizon flag is set, this represents the time at which tombstone records
     *       and aborted transaction markers should be removed.</li>
     * </ul></p>
     *
     * <p>Individual record timestamps are computed as: {@code baseTimestamp + timestampDelta}</p>
     *
     * @return the base timestamp in milliseconds since epoch, or -1 ({@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP})
     *         for empty batches without a delete horizon
     *
     * @see #maxTimestamp() for the batch's maximum timestamp
     * @see #deleteHorizonMs() to check if delete horizon flag is set
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
8. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();
        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);
        return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Creates an input stream for reading the compressed or uncompressed record data in this batch.
     *
     * <p>If the batch is compressed, the returned stream will decompress data on-read. For uncompressed
     * batches, the stream directly reads from the buffer.</p>
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier supplier for decompression buffers. For optimal performance with small batches,
     *        use a reusable buffer supplier (e.g., from a pool) to avoid repeated allocations of large
     *        buffers (e.g., 64 KB for LZ4).
     *
     * @return an input stream positioned at the start of record data in this batch
     *
     * @see Compression for details on compression types and buffer requirements
     */
    public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();
        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);
        return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier);
    }
9. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used only for reading or
 * modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer see {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder},
 * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants.
 */
public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {
After
/**
 * In-memory implementation of {@link Records} backed by a ByteBuffer.
 *
 * <p><b>Primary Use Cases:</b>
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Reading or modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches from the log or network.</li>
 *   <li>Iterating over batches and records for filtering, validation, or processing.</li>
 * </ul>
 * To create new records from scratch, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or factory methods like
 * {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}, {@link #withIdempotentRecords}, etc.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> This class is <b>not thread-safe</b>. The backing ByteBuffer and batch
 * objects can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates during log appending). External synchronization
 * is required for concurrent access. However, individual batch iteration is safe if the buffer contents
 * are not modified during iteration.</p>
 *
 * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b>
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Batch iteration is lazy—batches are parsed incrementally, not all at once.</li>
 *   <li>Record decompression is lazy—only decompressed when actually read via iterators.</li>
 *   <li>No copying of record data; all reads directly reference the backing buffer via slices.</li>
 *   <li>{@link #validBytes()} requires scanning all batches; the result is cached.</li>
 * </ul></p>
 *
 * <p><b>Buffer Management:</b> The ByteBuffer backing this instance can be in several states:
 * <ul>
 *   <li><b>Read-only:</b> Created via {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)}, position/limit managed externally.</li>
 *   <li><b>Writable:</b> Created via {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder#build()}, position and limit reflect valid data.</li>
 * </ul></p>
 *
 * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches
 * @see RecordBatch for batch-level operations
 * @see Record for record
... (truncated)
10. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this message set not including any partial, trailing messages. This
     * may be smaller than what is returned by {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     * @return The number of valid bytes
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
After
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this record collection that form complete, valid batches.
     *
     * <p>This may be smaller than {@link #sizeInBytes()} if the buffer contains incomplete or
     * trailing partial batches (e.g., during network reception). Incomplete batches are excluded
     * from this count but present in the buffer.</p>
     *
     * <p><b>Performance:</b> The first call requires scanning all batches to compute the total;
     * the result is cached for subsequent calls. If the batch iteration is expensive (e.g., large
     * record sets with many batches), consider caching the result at a higher level.</p>
     *
     * @return the number of bytes in complete, valid batches
     *
     * @see #sizeInBytes() for the total buffer size (including partial batches)
     * @see #batches() to iterate over individual batches
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
11. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Filter records based on retention rules, writing results to a destination buffer.
     *
     * <p>This method is used for log compaction and record expiration, applying batch and record-level
     * retention decisions via the provided {@code filter}. Batches can be entirely deleted or retained
     * (possibly with records removed). Empty batches may be retained to preserve producer state for
     * idempotent/transactional producers.</p>
     *
     * <p><b>Filtering Process:</b>
     * <ol>
     *   <li>Each batch is evaluated via {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)}.</li>
     *   <li>If batch retention is {@link RecordFilter.BatchRetention#DELETE}, the entire batch is skipped.</li>
     *   <li>Otherwise, each record is evaluated via {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}.</li>
     *   <li>Retained records are written to {@code destinationBuffer}, possibly in a new batch if
     *       modification was necessary.</li>
     * </ol></p>
     *
     * <p><b>Performance:</b> For compressed batches, this method decompresses records on-demand using
     * the supplied {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}. Reusable buffer suppliers significantly improve
     * performance for small batches where allocation dominates. Buffer overflow is handled transparently
     * by allocating larger buffers as needed.</p>
     *
     * @param filter filter defining batch and record retention logic
     * @param destinationBuffer buffer to write filtered records to. If overflow occurs, a larger buffer
     *        is allocated and returned in {@code FilterResult#outputBuffer()}
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier for decompression buffers. For performance with small
     *        batches, use {@link BufferSupplier#cr
... (truncated)
12. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        return new MemoryRecords(buffer);
    }
After
    /**
     * Create a {@code MemoryRecords} instance for reading existing record batches from a buffer.
     *
     * <p>The returned instance provides read-only access to record batches. The buffer is read from
     * its current position to its limit. For batch iteration, the buffer position is not modified;
     * a duplicate buffer is used internally to preserve the original state.</p>
     *
     * <p><b>Use Cases:</b> Reading records from the log, network buffer, or in-memory batch collections.
     * Use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or {@link #withRecords} variants to create new records.</p>
     *
     * @param buffer ByteBuffer containing serialized record batches. The buffer's position and limit
     *        define the readable range. The buffer must remain valid for the lifetime of the returned
     *        MemoryRecords instance.
     *
     * @return a MemoryRecords instance wrapping the buffer for reading batches
     *
     * @throws NullPointerException if buffer is null
     *
     * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder to create new records
     */
    public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        return new MemoryRecords(buffer);
    }
13. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                               Compression compression,
                                               TimestampType timestampType,
                                               long baseOffset) {
        return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset);
    }
After
    /**
     * Create a builder for constructing new record batches using the current record format version.
     *
     * <p>This convenience method uses {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2). For legacy
     * format support, use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)}.</p>
     *
     * <p>The builder initializes batches with:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>No producer ID (default non-idempotent producer)</li>
     *   <li>No transaction markers</li>
     *   <li>Current system time for {@code LOG_APPEND_TIME} timestamp type</li>
     * </ul></p>
     *
     * @param buffer ByteBuffer to write batches to. Must have sufficient remaining capacity for
     *        the records to be added. Can be passed to subsequent {@code builder()} calls to
     *        accumulate multiple batches.
     * @param compression compression type for the records
     * @param timestampType whether timestamps are CREATE_TIME (client) or LOG_APPEND_TIME (broker)
     * @param baseOffset offset of the first record in the batch
     *
     * @return a MemoryRecordsBuilder ready to append records
     *
     * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder#append for adding records to the batch
     * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder#build for finalizing the batch
     */
    public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                               Compression compression,
                                               TimestampType timestampType,
                                               long baseOffset) {
        return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset);
    }
14. EDIT /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) {
        return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records);
    }
After
    /**
     * Create a {@code MemoryRecords} instance from the provided records using default settings.
     *
     * <p>This is a convenience factory for creating record batches with:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (v2)</li>
     *   <li>Base Offset: 0</li>
     *   <li>Timestamp Type: CREATE_TIME</li>
     *   <li>No producer ID (non-idempotent)</li>
     *   <li>No transactions</li>
     * </ul></p>
     *
     * <p><b>Typical Use:</b> Creating test records or simple non-idempotent producer batches.</p>
     *
     * @param compression the compression type (NONE, SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD)
     * @param records the records to include in the batch
     *
     * @return a MemoryRecords instance containing a single batch with the provided records
     *
     * @throws IllegalStateException if records cannot fit in a single batch due to buffer size constraints
     *
     * @see #withIdempotentRecords for idempotent producer records
     * @see #withTransactionalRecords for transactional records
     */
    public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) {
        return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records);
    }
15. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Summary

This document summarizes the Javadoc additions made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`.

## Overview

Three core classes handle record batch serialization in Kafka:
1. **RecordBatch** - Interface defining batch operations
2. **DefaultRecordBatch** - Concrete implementation for magic v2+
3. **MemoryRecords** - In-memory collection and management of batches

## Class-Level Documentation Enhancements

### RecordBatch (Interface)

**Purpose:** Container and interface for Kafka message batches across all format versions.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly documented as NOT thread-safe (requires external synchronization)
- **Role in Protocol:** Explained fundamental importance in wire protocol and commit log format
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Lazy decompression for compressed batches (on-demand via iterators)
  - Direct buffer access for uncompressed data (no copying)
  - Enables idempotent producers, transactions, and log compaction features

**Cross-References:**
- {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for magic v2+ implementation
- {@link MemoryRecords} for batch collections
- {@link Record} for individual records

### DefaultRecordBatch (Concrete Implementation)

**Purpose:** Binary serialization and parsing of record batches in Kafka's v2+ format.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; state can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates)
- **Binary Format:** Documented full schema with field offsets and sizes
- **CRC Computation:**
  - Uses CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial
  - Covers attributes field through end of batch
  - Partition leader epoch excluded to avoid recomputation
- **Log Compaction Semantics:
... (truncated)

      

      

        Bash Commands (7)
        
1. $ git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/sg-evals/kafka--e678b4b.git kafka-repo 2>&1 | head -20
2. $ grep -n "public static MemoryRecords readableRecords" /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
3. $ grep -n "public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord" /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java | head -1
4. $ grep -A 25 "Gets the base timestamp of the batch" /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java | head -30
5. $ cat > /workspace/JAVADOC_CHANGES.md << 'EOF'
# Javadoc Changes Summary

## Files Modified

### 1. RecordBatch.java
- **Enhanced Class-Level Javadoc:**
  - Added thread-safety statement: "NOT thread-safe"
  - Documented role in Kafka protocol
  - Explained performance characteristics (lazy decompression)
  - Added cross-references to DefaultRecordBatch, MemoryRecords, Record

- **Enhanced Method Documentation:**
  - `isValid()` / `ensureValid()`: CRC-32C checksum explanation
  - `baseOffset()`: Performance implications and iteration cost
  - `streamingIterator()`: Buffer management, decompression costs, reusable buffers

### 2. DefaultRecordBatch.java
- **Comprehensive Class-Level Javadoc:**
  - Full binary schema with field types and annotations
  - Thread-safety: "NOT thread-safe" with in-place modification details
  - CRC-32C computation: polynomial, coverage, partition leader epoch exclusion
  - Log compaction semantics: sequence preservation, empty batch retention
  - Performance characteristics: header field accessibility, lazy decompression

- **Enhanced Method Documentation:**
  - `baseTimestamp()`: Timestamp delta calculation, delete horizon semantics, empty batch behavior
  - `recordInputStream()`: Decompression buffer requirements and reusability guidance

### 3. MemoryRecords.java
- **Detailed Class-Level Javadoc:**
  - Primary use cases: reading logs, network buffers, filtering
  - Thread-safety: "NOT thread-safe" with synchronization boundaries
  - Performance characteristics: lazy batch/record iteration, no copying, caching
  - Buffer management: read-only vs writable modes

- **Enhanced Factory Methods:**
  - `readableRecords()`: Purpose, buffer lifetime, exception handling
  - `builder()`: Configuration defaults, convenience for current magic version
  - `withRecords()`: Convenience factory for simple non-idempotent batches

- **Enhanced Instance Methods:**
  - `validBytes()`: Partial batch semantics, cache behavior, performance cost
  - `filterTo()`: Log compaction process, batch/record retention logic, decompression details

## Documentation Quality Standards Met

✓ **Thread-Safety (100% Coverage)**
  - Every class explicitly states thread-safety guarantee
  - Synchronized access requirements specified
  - Exception: Batch iteration is safe without sync if buffer not modified

✓ **Performance Documentation**
  - Lazy decompression and iteration patterns documented
  - Buffer allocation costs explained for decompression
  - Caching behavior and trade-offs noted
  - BufferSupplier guidance for optimization provided

✓ **Kafka Protocol Context**
  - Producer ID/epoch for idempotence explained
  - Sequence numbers for duplicate detection documented
  - CRC-32C for corruption detection detailed
  - Offset/sequence preservation during compaction explained

✓ **Cross-References**
  - {@link} tags connect related classes
  - RecordBatch ↔ DefaultRecordBatch ↔ MemoryRecords relationships shown
  - Method-to-method references (e.g., baseTimestamp → maxTimestamp)

✓ **Implementation Unchanged**
  - Documentation-only additions
  - No logic changes
  - No private method documentation (as required)

## Key Javadoc Patterns Applied

### Thread Safety Pattern
```java
/**
 * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> This class is <b>not thread-safe</b>. 
 * [External synchronization requirement details]</p>
 */
```

### Performance Pattern
```java
/**
 * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b>
 * <ul>
 *   <li>[Specific optimization or bottleneck]</li>
 * </ul></p>
 */
```

### Protocol Context Pattern
```java
/**
 * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> [Explain architectural importance]</p>
 */
```

## Verification Commands

### Check RecordBatch Javadoc
```bash
grep -A 20 "A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format" \
  /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
```

### Check DefaultRecordBatch Javadoc
```bash
grep -A 45 "Concrete implementation of" \
  /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
```

### Check MemoryRecords Javadoc
```bash
grep -A 30 "In-memory implementation of" \
  /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
```

## Documentation Output Location

- **Summary Document:** `/workspace/documentation.md`
- **Modified Source Files:** `/workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`
  - RecordBatch.java
  - DefaultRecordBatch.java
  - MemoryRecords.java

## Impact on Users

### For Log Reading
- Clear understanding of when batches are parsed (lazy iteration)
- Buffer reusability guidance for decompression efficiency
- Thread-safety boundaries documented

### For Record Production
- Factory method purposes and configurations explained
- Builder overload selection guidance
- Performance implications of different options

### For Log Compaction
- filterTo() semantics fully documented
- Batch/record retention logic explained
- Producer state preservation details provided

### For Correctness
- Sequence and offset preservation during compaction documented
- CRC integrity checking explained
- Timestamp semantic variations (CREATE_TIME vs LOG_APPEND_TIME) clarified
EOF
cat /workspace/JAVADOC_CHANGES.md

6. $ ls -lh /workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/*.java | grep -E "(RecordBatch|DefaultRecordBatch|MemoryRecords)" | head -10
